system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 5: Jazz Standard Study #4: It Could Happen to You

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 5 — Jazz Standard Study #4: It Could Happen to You

_Pages 70-86._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## target chord definition

> A target chord is sim-
> ply a chord within a progression that’s being targeted. In a ii-V-| chord
> progression, the target chord is the | chord. The ii and V are targeting
> the |, the arrival point.

**Significance (p. 78).** This passage defines the concept of target chords, which is the analytical lens the author uses throughout the chord analysis of the entire tune.

## harmony analysis approach

> Remember, the most important thing to pay attention to when trying
> to understand the harmony of a song is where the chords are going.
> If we look at a Gmin7(b5) and a C7(b9) as separate chords, they are
> confusing. But if we look at them as resolving to an Fmin7, then we
> have something functional to understand. Then we can look at the
> Fmin7 and ask how it functions in the overall tonality of the song.

**Significance (p. 79).** This passage states the author's foundational rule for harmonic analysis: always interpret chords in terms of their resolution target rather than in isolation.

## ii-V of diatonic chords

> Remember in our last study how we talked about secondary domi-
>
> nants? Well, it’s just as easy to add a ii chord in front of it to create a
> ii-V chord progression. This is precisely what is happening here.

**Significance (p. 78).** This passage gives the rule for how secondary dominants expand into ii-V pairs, which is the central harmonic mechanism driving most of this tune's chord changes.

## iii(b5)-VI7 reinterpretation

> If we want to relate the ii-V of ii directly to the parent tonal center,
> then what would those chords represent? The Gmin7(b5) can also be
> thought of as the iiib5 (adding a b5 to the iii minor) and a VI7.
>
> A dominant VI, as we’ve discussed before is not at all uncommon. On
> top of that, dominant chords are easy to alter, so ab? makes perfect
> sense considering the chord it is leading to. A illb5 is odd, but again,
> if we combine this understanding with the ii-V of ii concept everything
> ties together.

**Significance (p. 79).** This passage teaches a dual analytical reading of the same chord pair — as both a ii-V of ii and as iii(b5)-VI7 — equipping the reader to hear the same passage two functional ways simultaneously.

## improvisation as composition

> Improvising is often described as “composition sped up.” But if you
> want to compose quickly, wouldn't it be helpful to practice composing
> slowly (aka a normal pace)?

**Significance (p. 82).** This passage articulates the pedagogical rationale for writing contrafacts as an improvisation practice tool, justifying the entire improv lesson that follows.

## contrafact melody goals

> For this contrafact, | tried to keep the melody as simple as | could. My
> goal was to create a sing-able melody. This is the kind of contrafact
> | suggest you compose. Try to keep it simple. The exercise here is to
> create a strong melody that both outlines the chord changes appro-
> priately while expressing a theme.

**Significance (p. 83).** This passage gives the author's explicit compositional rule for student contrafacts: singability, simplicity, chord-tone outlining, and thematic expression.

## recurring themes and space

> 1. There are re-occurring rhythmic and melodic themes through-
> out with variations. These are good characteristics for any song
> to have, but ultimately you will want some of this in your improv
> too.
>
> 2. | leave space. There is room for the melody to breathe, and
> there also should be in your improvisation.

**Significance (p. 83).** These two points codify the author's rules for good melodic improvisation — thematic repetition with variation, and deliberate use of space — derived from analysis of his own contrafact example.

## Bebop style definition

> In case you aren’t familiar, Be-
> bop is a style in jazz developed in the 1940’s by pioneers like Charlie
> Parker and Dizzy Gillespie. A lot of contrafacts were written in this era,
> and they tend to be busier, less sing-able and similar to a solo.

**Significance (p. 84).** This passage defines the Bebop style as applied to contrafact writing, distinguishing it from the singable approach and setting expectations for the second contrafact example.

## Bebop contrafact structure rule

> This may even seem to you like a full-on bebop solo, as is the nature
> of bebop heads, but note the themes throughout. There are repeated
> sections, rhythmic clones and general structure. So, if you do go this
> route with your contrafact, make sure you include all of these elements.

**Significance (p. 85).** This passage gives the rule that even a busy Bebop-style contrafact must contain repeated sections, rhythmic clones, and deliberate structure rather than being purely stream-of-consciousness.

## minor iv chord function

> In bar 10 there are two common sets of chord progressions. The most
> basic way to approach it is with a V chord (Bb7) leading into the |. But
> often times, musicians will play an Abmin6, which functions as the mi-
> nor IV chord. In our All of Me study, we talked about how you can per-
> ceive the minor tv as a quick jump into the minor tonic key.
>
> You can also turn that Abminé into a ii-V (Abmin7-Db7), or you can
> even just play a Db7, which | do in some upcoming examples.

**Significance (p. 80).** Defines the minor iv chord substitution and gives practical variants (Abmin6, Abmin7-Db7, or plain Db7), teaching the player how to choose among standard substitutions at this specific moment in the form.

## contrafact definition

> A contrafact is a melody that is written over top of an existing songs
> chord changes, thus creating a new song altogether. Classic examples
> of this are rhythm changes heads like “Oleo” or “Anthropology.” Those
> are melodies written over the changes to Gershwin’'s “| Got Rhythm.”

**Significance (p. 81).** Formally defines the contrafact concept, which is the core compositional technique taught in the improv lesson of this chapter.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
